facts_disclosed: Mother Dona Lucia visiting Dubai for two weeks starting March 15, 2025

turns.content: Okay I need help planning something and I'm SO excited I can barely type straight haha!!
I love the energy! What are you planning? Let's hear it.

My mom is coming to Dubai!!! She arrives March 15th and she's staying for two whole weeks! Ai meu Deus I'm going to cry at the airport I already know it. I haven't seen her since September and two weeks together is the longest she's ever visited. I need to plan EVERYTHING so she has the best time ever.
That's wonderful! Two weeks is a great amount of time — long enough to really settle in and not feel rushed. Has she visited Dubai before, or is this her first time?

She came once before, three years ago, but only for five days and she was so jet-lagged half the time haha. She's Dona Lucia — she's 68, she has energy but she's not going to do crazy adventure stuff. She likes beautiful things, good food, taking her time. And she wants to see WHERE I live, like really understand my daily life here. She always says on our video calls "Bruna I can't picture it, show me for real!"

That's sweet — she wants to understand your world here, not just see tourist landmarks. For a two-week trip with a 68-year-old who appreciates beauty and food and wants to experience your real life, I'd suggest splitting it into three phases:

**Week 1: Your Dubai** — Show her your daily routine. Take her to your clinic, walk her through your neighborhood, go to your regular restaurants, introduce her to your friends. Let her live your life with you.

**Week 2: Tourist Dubai** — The highlights she should see: Burj Khalifa, Dubai Mall, the souks, a desert safari (a gentle one), maybe a day trip to Abu Dhabi for the Grand Mosque.

**Sprinkled throughout: Food experiences** — Since she loves food, find the best Brazilian restaurant in Dubai for a homesick-cure dinner, plus take her to try Arabic food, brunch culture, everything.

Does that kind of structure work, or does she have specific things she's asked to do?

Oh I LOVE this structure!! The "Your Dubai" week is perfect because that's exactly what she wants. She wants to meet my friends, she wants to see my apartment, she wants to come to the clinic and watch me work haha she's so proud she tells everyone her daughter is a doctor — I'm a PT not a doctor but try telling a Brazilian mother that!!

Ha! That's such a universal mom thing — close enough, you're a doctor to her. Bringing her to the clinic is a great idea, even if it's just for a quick visit. Seeing you in your professional element will mean the world to her.

For the food side — do you know any good Brazilian restaurants in Dubai? That could be a special night out, maybe in the first few days when she's settling in and might be craving something familiar.

There's a place called Brasileirinho in JBR that's decent — not like real Brazilian food obviously, nothing compares to my mom's cooking haha — but the feijoada is respectable. I was thinking we go there early in the visit. AND I want to take her to an Arabic restaurant because she's never had Middle Eastern food! She saw me eating hummus on a video call once and she was like "what IS that" haha. Dona Lucia has never had hummus!! Can you imagine??

A hummus revelation is a beautiful thing — she's going to love it! For Arabic food, consider taking her to Al Fanar in Festival City for a traditional Emirati experience, or Arabian Tea House in Al Fahidi for a really beautiful setting with authentic food. The Al Fahidi neighborhood itself is gorgeous and walkable, which would be perfect for her pace — old Dubai, wind towers, art galleries.

For the desert safari, look into a morning one rather than evening — less intense heat, and she'll be fresher. Some companies do gentle experiences with camel rides and breakfast rather than the dune-bashing thrill rides.

Oh no dune bashing — Dona Lucia would have a heart attack haha!! A gentle morning one with camels sounds perfect. She loves animals. And the Al Fahidi area is such a good idea, she would love the old architecture. She's always complaining that Dubai is too modern and shiny from the photos I send — I need to show her the historic side!

Al Fahidi will completely change her perception — the narrow lanes, traditional buildings, and creek-side views are a world away from the skyscraper Dubai she's seen in your photos. You could do a full afternoon there: walk the neighborhood, have lunch at Arabian Tea House, then take an abra (water taxi) across the creek to the spice and gold souks. The whole experience costs almost nothing and it's incredibly atmospheric. Your mom will love the souks especially — the colors, the smells, the haggling.

Oh gente she is going to go CRAZY at the gold souk. Dona Lucia loves gold jewelry — every Brazilian woman does haha. I'm going to have to physically drag her out of there. And the spice souk! She cooks every single day, she'll want to buy every spice they have. I need to budget for souk shopping because she will not hold back!!

Smart to budget ahead! The spice souk is very affordable though — saffron, dried lime, za'atar, sumac — she can stock up for a fraction of what those would cost in Brazil. The gold souk is where you'll need the budget! But even window shopping there is an experience.

One more idea: since she's staying two weeks and you'll be working during the day for at least some of it, maybe set her up with a couple of easy solo activities for those times? Things like the Dubai Mall aquarium, a spa day, or a hop-on-hop-off bus that she could manage on her own while you're at the clinic.

That's smart because I was worried about leaving her alone during work days. She's independent but she doesn't speak English very well. A spa day she can handle — no language needed when someone is giving you a massage haha! And maybe I can take a few days off too. I have vacation days saved up. Okay I'm going to make a spreadsheet now — day by day, everything organized. Ai meu Deus March 15th cannot come fast enough!! Thank you querida, you've given me so many good ideas!

A spreadsheet is the way to go — with two weeks you have plenty of room to balance planned activities with relaxed days. Don't overschedule though; some of the best moments will be the unplanned ones, like cooking together in your kitchen or just sitting on your balcony talking. Enjoy every minute with Dona Lucia — March 15th will be here before you know it!
